text: The surgeon inspects the bowel, marking areas with visible strictures for potential strictureplasty or resection. A tight stricture is noted at the ileocecal valve, correlating with MRI findings.

The surgeon inspects the bowel, identifying and marking areas of fibrous stenotic Crohn's disease with visible strictures. The ileocecal valve is noted to have a tight stricture, correlating with MRI findings, prompting the plan for an ileocolic resection.

The surgeon identifies a Meckel's diverticulum adjacent to a Crohn's disease stricture and begins mobilizing the mesentery in this area. The iliocolic pedicle is tented, and a medial to lateral dissection is performed. The surgeon isolates the artery and vein separately, thinning out the pedicle. The colon mesentery is taken, preserving the right colics, and the dissection proceeds to the junction of the ascending colon and cecum. Lateral mobilization joins the previously completed medial dissection.

The surgeon identifies a Meckel's diverticulum adjacent to a Crohn's disease stricture and begins mobilizing the mesentery around the Meckel's. The iliocolic pedicle is tented, and a medial to lateral dissection is initiated. The surgeon thins out the pedicle, isolating the iliocolic artery and vein, and takes them separately during the mesenteric resection.

The surgeon performs an ileocolic resection, focusing on the colon mesentery while preserving the right colic vessels. The dissection progresses to the junction of the ascending colon and cecum, followed by lateral mobilization that connects with the previously completed medial dissection. The hepatic flexure is not mobilized to minimize scar tissue at the ileocolic anastomosis site.

The surgeon performs an intracorporeal anastomosis without mobilizing the hepatic flexure, finishing the terminal ileum mesentery and checking perfusion. Using cautery to mark division points, the surgeon staples the small bowel from the suprapubic port, creating an isoperistaltic ileocolic anastomosis. A colotomy and enterotomy are made, followed by additional stapling to extend the anastomosis. The common channel is sutured closed in a two-layer fashion, starting at the near corner and imbricating back down.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the terminal ileum mesentery and checks for fusion using cautery marks as guides. A stapler is inserted through the suprapubic port to divide the small bowel and perform an isoperistaltic ileocolic anastomosis. The surgeon makes a colotomy and enterotomy, adjusting the anastomosis size with additional stapler firings. The common channel is sutured closed in a two-layer fashion, starting at the near corner and imbricating back towards the far corner.

The surgeon performs a small bowel resection, taking the mesentery to the Meckel's diverticulum back to the bowel wall, identifying it as the distal margin. A window into the true small bowel mesentery is created, and the surgeon moves proximally to the area of disease. The Meckel's diverticulum, scarred to the retroperitoneum and appearing abnormally thickened, is resected. A side-to-side antiparastaltic anastomosis is lined up, with otomies made and a stapler used to create a wide anastomosis. The common channel is sewn closed using a baseball stitch, starting at the near corner and running out and around.

The surgeon performs a small bowel resection by taking the mesentery to the Meckel's diverticulum back to the bowel wall, identifying it as the distal margin. A window into the true small bowel mesentery is created, and the surgeon moves proximally to the area of disease. The Meckel's diverticulum, which appears scarred to the retroperitoneum with an abnormal thickened appearance, is resected due to its potential role in obstructions and confirmed Crohn's disease involvement.

The surgeon aligns the small bowel for a side-to-side antiparastaltic anastomosis, creating enterotomies. A stapler is used to form a wide anastomosis, followed by suturing the common channel closed with a baseball stitch, starting at the near corner and running over the top.

The surgeon performs an inverting Lembert suture technique to secure the ileocolic anastomosis, visually confirming the separation of the anastomosis sites by approximately two feet. The surgical field is covered with omentum, and specimens are extracted through the suprapubic port site.

The video shows the final inspection of the surgical site, highlighting the ileocolic anastomosis, which is separated by approximately two feet from another anastomosis. The surgeon covers the area with omentum, and the specimens are extracted through the suprapubic port site.
